analogy: Think of "How When and Where" through the chapter's own opening images: Introduction: How, When and Where 1 How Important are Dates? There was a time when historians were fascinated with dates. There were heated debates about the dates on which rulers were crowned or battles were fought. In the common-sense notion, history was synonymous with dates. The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
How When and Where is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that introduction: How, When and Where 1 How Important are Dates?

The text explains that there was a time when historians were fascinated with dates.

The text explains that there were heated debates about the dates on which rulers were crowned or battles were fought.

The text explains that in the common-sense notion, history was synonymous with dates.

The text explains that you may have heard people say, “I find history boring because it is all about memorising dates.” Is such a conception true?

The text explains that history is certainly about changes that occur over time.

The text explains that it is about finding out how things were in the past and how things have changed.

The text explains that as soon as we compare the past with the present, we refer to time, we talk of “before” and “after”.

The text explains that living in the world we do not always ask historical questions about what we see around us.

The text explains that we take things for granted, as if what we see has always been in the world we inhabit.

The text explains that but most of us have our moments of wonder, when we are curious, and we ask questions that actually are historical.

The text explains that watching someone sip a cup of tea at a roadside tea stall, you may wonder – when did people begin to drink tea or coffee?

The text explains that looking out of the window of a train you may ask yourself – when were railways built and how did people travel long distances before the age of railways?

The text explains that reading the newspaper in the morning you may be curious to know how people got to hear about things before newspapers began to be printed.

The text explains that 1 – Brahmans offering the Shastras to Britannia, frontispiece to the first map produced by James Rennel, 1782 Rennel was asked by Robert Clive to produce maps of Hindustan.

The text explains that an enthusiastic supporter of British conquest of India, Rennel saw preparation of maps as essential to the process of domination.

The text explains that what sources do historians use in writing about the last 250 years of Indian history?

The text explains that administration produces records One important source is the official records of the British administration.

The text explains that you can also study the notes and reports that district officials prepared, or the instructions and directives that were sent by officials at the top to provincial administrators.

The text explains that source 1 Reports to the Home Department I n 1 9 4 6 t h e c o l o n i a l government in India was trying to put down a mutiny that broke out on the ships of the Royal Indian Navy.

The text explains that when we begin to search for these other sources, we find them in plenty, though they are more difficult to get than official records.

The text explains that all these sources, however, were produced by those who were literate.

The text explains that even cattle would not eat the chappattis and dal which we have to eat.” Hindustan Times, 22 March, 1946 Source 2 Look at Sources 1 and 2.

The text explains that (b) Official documents help us understand what the people of the country think.

The text explains that (c) The British thought surveys were important for effective administration.

The text explains that what is the problem with the periodisation of Indian history that James Mill offers?

The text explains that why did the British preserve official documents?

The text explains that how will the information historians get from old newspapers be different from that found in police reports?

The text explains that can you think of examples of surveys in your world today?

The text explains that think about how toy companies get information about what young people enjoy playing with or how the government finds out about the number of young people in school.

The text explains that what can a historian derive from such surveys?

The text explains that let’s imagine Imagine that you are a historian wanting to find out about how agriculture changed in a remote tribal area after independence.

The text explains that list the different ways in which you would find information on this.

Source: NCERT Class 8 Our Pasts III, Chapter 1: How When and Where

cases.name: People
cases.point: People in India did not begin drinking tea one fine day; they developed a taste for it over time. (NCERT Class 8 Our Pasts III Ch1 pg 1)

cases.name: In
cases.point: In the histories written by British historians in India, the rule of each Governor- General was important. (NCERT Class 8 Our Pasts III Ch1 pg 1)

schemes.name: The British
schemes.point: The British believed that the act of writing was important. (NCERT Class 8 Our Pasts III Ch1 pg 4)

schemes.name: Every
schemes.point: Every instruction, plan, policy decision, agreement, investigation had to be clearly written up. (NCERT Class 8 Our Pasts III Ch1 pg 4)

schemes.name: By
schemes.point: By the middle of the nineteenth century, with the spread of printing, multiple copies of these records were printed as proceedings of each government department. (NCERT Class 8 Our Pasts III Ch1 pg 4)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 2 OUR PASTS – III and INTRODUCTION: HOW, WHEN AND WHERE 3 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to How When and Where; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain How When and Where through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 2 OUR PASTS – III and INTRODUCTION: HOW, WHEN AND WHERE 3 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

options: The picture here tries to suggest that Indians willingly gave over their ancient texts to Britannia – the symbol of British power – as...
2 OUR PASTS – III All such historical questions refer us back to notions of time.
But time does not have to be always precisely dated in terms of a particular year or a month.
Sometimes it is actually incorrect to fix precise dates to processes that happen over a period of time.
